coredata.dc:title: Luminescent carbon dots obtained from cellulose
coredata.dc:creator: da Silva Souza, Débora Rosa
Caminhas, Larissa Durães
de Mesquita, João Paulo
Pereira, Fabiano Vargas
coredata.dc:description: In this work we have prepared carbon dots (CD) from bleached eucalyptus kraft pulp through a simple method using sulfuric and nitric acid. The obtained CD were fully characterized by several techniques including FTIR, Elemental Analysis, XRD, Raman, HRTEM, UV–Vis, Fluorescence and Potentiometric titration. The diameter of the quasi-spherical biomass-derived CD ranged from 1 to 3 nm, with an average size of 2 nm and a great amount of different oxygen groups (mainly carboxylic acids) were detected on the surface. This considerable amount of carboxylic acid was used to successfully passivate the nanoparticles with oligomeric PEG1500 N molecules to enhance the quantum yield of the CD. Considering that the CD were obtained by a low cost method and that the renewable precursor is highly abundant, this method can represent an important strategy to reduce the final cost for the production of carbon nanoparticles with potential applications in different areas such as biomedical and photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cellulose
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chemical modification of the carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM of the CDotCel in different magnifications (a,b,c) and the histogram showing the distribution particle size (d).
Raman spectra comparison of cellulose and CDotCel.
FTIR spectra of cellulose and CDotCel.

UV–vis absorption spectra for CdotCel sample in water (a). Fluorescence spectra obtained from different wavelengths of excitation, i. e, 320–480 nm (with 20 nm increments starting from 320 nm). The inset in (a) shows the CdotCel sample solution in water irradiated with UV light (365 nm) source.
Surface chemical modification with PEG1500 N by amine and mainly amide linkages.
FTIR spectra of PEG1500 N (a), CDotCel(b) and CDotCelPEG (c).
TEM of the CDotCelPEG sample in different magnifications (a,b) and the histogram showing the distribution particle size for the same sample (c).

Fluorescence spectra of CDotCelPEG obtained from different wavelengths of excitation, i. e, 320–480 nm (with 20 nm increments starting from 320 nm). The inset shows an aqueous solution of the same sample illuminated with a UV lamp (365 nm).
Results obtained by fitting experimental titration curves of CDotCel.

head.ce:title: Luminescent carbon dots obtained from cellulose

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Universidade Federal dos Vales do Jequitinhonha e Mucuri, Rodovia MGT 367 - Km 583, nº 5000, Alto da Jacuba, Diamantina, MG CEP 39100-000, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Universidade Federal dos Vales do Jequitinhonha e Mucuri

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Universidade Federal de Minas Gerais, Av. Antônio Carlos, 6627 - Pampulha, Belo Horizonte, MG CEP 31270-901, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work we have prepared carbon dots (CD) from bleached eucalyptus kraft pulp through a simple method using sulfuric and nitric acid. The obtained CD were fully characterized by several techniques including FTIR, Elemental Analysis, XRD, Raman, HRTEM, UV–Vis, Fluorescence and Potentiometric titration. The diameter of the quasi-spherical biomass-derived CD ranged from 1 to 3 nm, with an average size of 2 nm and a great amount of different oxygen groups (mainly carboxylic acids) were detected on the surface. This considerable amount of carboxylic acid was used to successfully passivate the nanoparticles with oligomeric PEG1500 N molecules to enhance the quantum yield of the CD. Considering that the CD were obtained by a low cost method and that the renewable precursor is highly abundant, this method can represent an important strategy to reduce the final cost for the production of carbon nanoparticles with potential applications in different areas such as biomedical and photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CD) were obtained for the first time in 2004, during eletrophoretic purification of single-wall carbon nanotubes [1]. They represent a relatively new class of nanocarbons presenting a quasi-spherical shape, normally with a size between 2 and 10 nm, with a predominant graphitic character (sp2 carbon) merged by sp3-type carbon inclusions [2].

These carbon nanoparticles present some interesting properties such as high specific surface area, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, low cost, and fluorescent properties. In addition, they present a surface that can be easily passivate to achieve specific functionalities [3].

All these properties make them promising materials for applications in different areas such as in optolectronic devices, biosensors, photocatalysis, solar cells, and applications in bioimaging, such as in imunofluorescence. Recently, surface-modified CD have been synthesized for ultrasensitive detection of mercury ions (Hg2+), in concentration as low as 18 pM [4].

The CD can be obtained from different carbon sources using different approaches, normally classified as “top-down” and “bottton-up” strategies. The first methods used were mainly focused in top-down strategies, where the carbon sources, such as carbon nanotubes [5] or graphite [6] were broken down using different methodologies such as laser ablation, arch discharge and electrochemical oxidation. On the other hand, the CD can also be obtained from small precursor molecules (bottom-up approach), using for example citrate [7] or ascorbic acid [8], by different heat treatments such as pyrolysis, hydrothermal carbonization or microwave-assisted methods.

Another strategy that, in some terms could be classified as botom-up, is the use of biomass sources, such as chitosan or lignocellulosic materials, as precursor material. The use of biomass to produce CD can represent advantages related to the high availability and low cost of the final material, mainly if the synthetic strategy does not require expensive material or elaborate experimental set-ups.

In this work, we have used cellulose as precursor for the preparation of CD. Considering that cellulose is the most abundant polymer in nature, the use of precursor such as cellulose may represent an important strategy to reduce the final cost of producing carbon nanoparticles.

In addition, using a relatively low temperature and a experimental set-up that did not require a pressurized vessel, we have obtained CD using a two step method using sulfuric and nitric acid to obtain CD with a large amount of carboxylic acid on the surface. This great amount of carboxylic acid was used to subsequently passivate the nanoparticles to enhance the quantum yield, allowing its potential application in bioimaging.

Eucalyptus kraft wood pulp was used as precursor for the carbon dots. Sulfuric acid, nitric acid and potassium carbonate were purchased from Synth (São Paulo, Brazil). Thionyl chloride (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, USA) was distilled immediately before use whereas PEG1500 (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, USA) was used as received.

The carbon dots were prepared using a method described in the literature to prepare oxidized CD from carbohydrates, with some modifications [9]. Firstly, 5 g of eucalyptus kraft wood pulp was dispersed in 20 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid at 80 °C under strong magnetic stirring. The cellulose is dehydrated producing carbonaceous materials and after 40 min, the reaction was interrupted by adding 120 mL of water. The obtained carbon material was washed using vacuum filtration until the excess of acid was removed. Then, the material was dried in an oven at 60 °C. Subsequently the oxidation step of the carbon was carried out adding the obtained material in 125 mL of nitric acid (2 M). This mixture was refluxed for 12 h, neutralized with 34.5 g of potassium carbonate and finally dialyzed using a cellulose membrane (molecular weight cut-off ∼ 12,000), until pH ∼7. This sample was namely CDotCEL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical modification of the carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The CDOtCEL sample was modified using oligomeric PEG1500 N molecules as described elsewhere with minor modifications [10]. Firstly, 25 mg of freeze-dried CDotCEL sample was added to 5 mL of thionyl chloride (previously distilled) and refluxed for 6 h. Afterward, the thionyl chloride was removed under reduced pressure and 250 mg of PEG1500 N (previously dried) was added to the reaction medium. The system was heated to 110 °C and the reflux maintained for 72 h, in a closed system under nitrogen. The reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature, dispersed in water and then centrifuged to retain the supernatant (the pellet was the unreacted PEG1500 N). The sample prepared using this methodology was called CDotCelPEG.
FTIR spectra were obtained in a Perkim Elmer Spectrum RXI spectrometer in a frequence range of 4000–400 cm−1. The samples were prepared using the KBr disk method.

The Raman spectra were obtained using a Horiba Jobin-Yvon system with a 514 nm excitation wavelength. The measurements of powder samples were made with a powder adaptor and using a 50× objective lens with a laser power of 2 mW, and an exposure time of 2–10 s.

The X-ray diffractograms were obtained on a SHIMADZU XRD6000 diffractometer using Cu-Kα (λ = 0.154 nm) radiation filtered with Ni. The analyses were performed with 30 kV voltage and 30 mA current. The angle of incidence ranged from 5 to 70° 2θ with a speed of 2ºmin-1 and a resolution of 0.02°.

Potentiometric titration curves were performed at room temperature, under N2 atmosphere, using a SCHOTT automatic titrator (Titroline 7000). In a typical procedure, 5.0 mg of the sample was dispersed in 20 mL HCl solution (1.0 × 10−3 mol L−1) in an electrochemical cell followed by titration with a NaOH solution (0.012 mol L−1) CO2 free. The ionic strengths of both solutions were adjusted to 0.10 mol L−1 by dissolution of sodium chloride. A nonlinear method was employed to fit the acid-base potentiometric titration data to determine the nature and quantify the functional groups from their acid strength (pKa) on the surface of the carbon [11].
Elemental analysis was performed using a CHN Perkin-Elmer analyzer.
Thermogravimetric analysis (TG/DTG) was carried out using a DTG60 SHIMADZU. The samples were heated from 25 to 750 °C using a heating ramp of 10 °C.min−1 under N2 flow (200 mL min−1).
Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded using aTecnai G2-20 - SuperTwin FEI with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV.
The absorption spectra were obtained on a Varian Cary 100 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer.

In order to calculate the quantum yield, different concentrations of the sample were prepared in water, and the absorbance and fluorescence spectra were recorded (at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm). The integrated fluorescence intensity obtained for the sample as a function of absorbance was compared to the standard (quinine sulfate) using the equation [12]:
(1)

∅
S T

S

)

η
C D
2

2
)
In this equation, ϕ is the quantum yield, S is the slope, η is the refractive index and the subscript CD and ST is respectively, the carbon dot and the standard.

During the reaction between cellulose fibers with sulfuric acid, a very complex chemical reactions occurs involving a simultaneous combination of dehydration, polymerization (condensation and addition) and aromatization, leading to the formation of the carbon material, with significant amounts of sulfonic groups [13,14]. In the presence of a strong oxidant, i.e. HNO3, the formation of carbon nanostructures occurs as a consequence of the oxidative attack on the surface of the carbon particles, which promotes corrosion and the introduction of oxygenated functional groups such as –COOH, -C=O, -O-H and -NO2 [11,15].

Fig. 1 (a–c) shows typical TEM images of the CDotCel obtained at different magnifications. The images were obtained from a diluted solution (∼0.1%) and showed some isolated carbon dots with quasi-spherical morphology and also some agglomeration of nanoparticles. Analysis of a series of images has shown that the size of the carbon dots range from 1 to 3 nm, with an average size of 2 nm (histogram in Fig. 1d). The size of the CD obtained in this work is in agreement with some reports in the literature, mainly those using small molecules as precursor [7]. However, the average size of the CD obtained here is smaller compared with other carbon nanoparticles obtained from different biomass precursor, such as chitosan or chitin [16].

The HRTEM image showed in Fig. 1c indicated that the CDotCel nanoparticles have a crystalline structure with an interplanar distance of 0.29 nm (determined using Gatan software), which are similar to the lattice spacing of the [002] planes of graphitic materials (Fig. 1c).

The structure of the nanoparticles was also investigated with X-ray diffraction and Raman scattering spectroscopy. Fig. S1 shows the X-ray diffraction profiles of cellulose and CDotCel. The X-ray diffraction profile obtained from cellulose shows the main characteristic peaks of cellulose I at 2θ angle of 15° [101], 22.5° [002] and 34.5° [040], [17]. On the other hand, the X-ray diffraction profile obtained from the CDotCel shows a broad diffraction peak with a maximum around 2θ = 27°. This indicates first the destruction of the typical crystalline structure of cellulose during the reaction steps and also the appearance of the characteristic peak of the graphite (002) plane. This characteristic broad peak in carbon dots has been also attributed to disordered amorphous carbon phase [18–20].
he XRD patterns of the CDs (Fig. 1 c) also displayed.
a broad peak centered at 25.
8 (0.34 nm), which is also.
attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms.

The results obtained with Raman spectroscopy indicate similar observations, but with a greater clarity on the structural characteristics of the CDs (Fig. 2 ). Raman spectra of cellulose shows typical bands attributed to angular deformation in the cellulosic structure located around 950-570 cm−1. In the region between 1095 and 1122 cm−1, peaks related to axial deformation of C-O are observed [21] whereas angular deformation modes of methylene and hydroxyl are detected around 1200 to 1500 cm−1. Axial deformation of CH and CH2 is detected at 2895 cm−1.

The Raman spectrum of the CDotCel presented an intense band at 1580 cm−1 (G band) related to well-organized carbon and in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon, and another band at 1340 cm−1 (D band), associated to more defective carbons. The ID/IG ratio can be used as a measure of the degree of graphitization. In our case, the ID/IG ratio was 0.46 suggesting the presence of surface defects which can be related to the high amount of oxygenated groups on the CDs surface [22].

The functional groups were characterized by different techniques. Table S1 shows the results of elemental analysis obtained from CDotCel comparing with cellulose. The amount of carbon on these carbon dots is relatively low (42.16%, by weight) comparing with some values typically found to carbon dots [2]. Also, the amount of carbon and oxygen comparing the precursor with the CDotCel are similar, indicating that the dehydration and oxidation process of eucalyptus cellulose have formed carbon dots with a proportional number of oxygenated functional groups, replacing the hydroxyl groups of cellulose by different oxygen groups such as carboxylic acids, lactones and phenol. As expected, the amount of nitrogen increased due to the oxidation by nitric acid in the second step of the cellulose carbonization.

FTIR was used to qualitatively characterize the chemical structure of the obtained carbon materials and also to compare the presence of different functional groups with the precursor after carbonization. Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra of the precursor cellulose and the carbon material obtained after carbonization of cellulose.

The spectrum of cellulose showed typical absorptions of cellulose I. The band at 3390 cm−1 is attributed to the O-H stretching vibration, the bands centered at 2900 and 1431 cm−1 are characteristic of C-H stretching and -CH2 bending, respectively. The bands at 1160 and 1070 cm−1 are attributed to the saccharide structure.

After dehydration and oxidation of cellulose, significant changes are observed in the FTIR spectra, together with the destruction of the saccharide structure. The bands at 1120 and 1040 cm−1 can be attributed mainly to the C-O of different functional groups and the band at 1373 cm−1 is characteristic to N-O, mainly NO2. Also, C-H deformation of aromatics is observed at 795 cm−1 and an intense and broad absorption is observed between 1500 and 1850 cm−1. This absorptions are due to different bands which arise from C=C of aromatic structures, C=O of quinone and C=O from various functional groups such as carboxyl and anhydride (∼1770 and 1700 cm−1). Finally, the band at 3340 cm−1 is attributed to the O-H stretching vibration on the surface of the carbon structures.

A more detailed and quantitative analysis about the presence of oxygenated functional groups on the surface of the CDotCel was carried out using potentiometric titration. Fig. S2 shows the potentiometric titration curves obtained for CDotCel sample and Table 1 shows the amount of each functional group present on the surface of the carbon dots.

These results indicate a material with a surface heterogeneity, presenting different acidic functional groups. According to the potentiometric titration, 77% of the groups present on the CDOtCel surface presented pKa<5, typically attributed to carboxylic acids, 14% presented 6 < pKa<8, attributed to lactones, and 9% presented pKa>8, normally attributed to phenolic groups. These results are in accordance with the FTIR results which showed different oxygenated functional groups on CDotCel. For some applications like in biomaging, the large amount of oxygenated functional groups can represent an appreciable advantage because the obtained carbon dots are, in fact, highly hydrophilic and can be easily dispersed in physiological medium.

The optical properties of the carbon nanostructures in general are highly complex [24]. However, there is a consensus that they are related to the π structure of the basal planes (core) and functional groups. This statement has been effectively shown by different surface modification protocols, for example, reduction with hydrazine, which can enhances the integrity of surface π electron network [25].

The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4 a) of the sample prepared here presented a high optical absorption in the UV region that extended to ∼500 nm. It is possible to observe a shoulder around ∼300 nm, which has been assigned to the n-π∗transitions of C=O chromophore of different functional groups widely available in the structure of the CDs. Similarly, a shoulder around ∼250 nm due to the aromatic π-π∗ transition of the conjugated C=C [26]. The inset in Fig. 4a shows a CDotCel solution in water irradiated with UV light (365 nm) source.

Fig. 4b shows the photoluminescence spectra at different wavelengths excitation for the CDotCel sample dispersed in water. Carbon nanostructures prepared in this work showed strong emission in the blue/green (from 450 to 550 nm) region of the electromagnetic spectrum with excitation at wavelengths from 320 (black line) to 480 nm (dark blue line). The peaks emission maxima shift to higher wavelengths as the excitation wavelengths increase from 320 nm to 480 nm due to the excitation-dependent emission of carbon dots. There is a debate in the literature to explain this important characteristic found in CD [2,3,27]. The main explanation is that excitation-dependent emission occurs mainly due to different surface groups found on CD, which introduce trapping states with a series of energy levels. Also, this can occur due to optical selection of nanoparticles with different sizes (quantum effect) [28].

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the CdotCel sample was calculated using quinine sulfate as standard and using the method described in the literature as the slope method [12]. The calculated quantum yield of the unmodified carbon dot from eucalyptus source was 1.2%.

Several applications of the carbon dots require the development of nanoparticles with relatively high quantum yield. It was described in different papers that the insertion of different defects on the CD surface can enhance the quantum yield. Although the mechanism of the photoluminescence is not yet fully understood, the passivation by nitrogen-doping have been described as an important strategy to enhance the QY. Here, the CDotCel sample was modified using oligomeric PEG1500 N molecules, replacing the -COOH and -OH groups with amides and amines without significantly changing the π structure.

We have chosen this strategy due to the large amount of carboxylic acid groups on the CD surface, which can be used as a chemical platform for the introduction of new chemical functionalities by amine and amide linkages. The surface chemical modification with PEG1500 N by amine and mainly amide linkages was obtained after reaction with thionyl chloride, as described in the experimental section and showed in Fig. 5 .

The chemical modification of the CDotCel was confirmed by FTIR. Fig. 6 shows the infrared spectra obtained for the sample CDotCel and CDotCelPEG. The success of functionalization by analyzing the data obtained with this technique can be confirmed from the changes in the absorption region of the amide functional group and typical vibrations of -CH2 and C-O groups.

As expected, the FTIR spectrum of the CDotCelPEG sample shows significant changes in the characteristic regions of the above-mentioned functional groups. The appearance of the absorption band at 1650 cm−1 is characteristic of C=O stretching of amide. Also, the spectrum of the modified CdotCel sample exhibited characteristics bands of C-H stretching at 2900 cm−1, and bands between 1300 and 1450 cm−1 (scissoring and bending of –CH3 and –CH2 groups). In addition, characteristic bands of C-O-C ether (stretch) were observed at 1050-1150 cm−1.

Fig. 7 shows characteristics TEM images of the CdotCelPEG in two different magnifications. Using several TEM images, the mean values for the diameter of the modified-carbon dots were estimated to be in the range 1–5 nm, with an average of 2.8 nm, indicating a small increase in the mean values of the CD size. It can be associated with the coupling of the PEG chains on the CD surface. Also, the maintenance of the CD morphology means the chemical modification took place only onto the surface of the nanoparticles.

The photoluminescence spectra of the CDotCelPEG solution in water with different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 8 . The sample exhibited similar fluorescence emissions in a function of the excitation energy compared to the unmodified carbon dots: the peaks emissions shift linearly to higher wavelengths as the excitation wavelengths increase. CDotCelPEG sample showed similar absorption spectrum of the CDotCel, presenting a large absorption band around 320 nm.
The inset in Fig. 8 shows a photograph of the CDotCelPEG solution in water irradiated with UV light (365 nm) source.

In order to measure the quantum yield of the modified-carbon dots, fluorescence analyses were also carried out together with absorbance measurements of the CDtoCelPEG sample and the quinine sulfate standard. The quantum yield of the CdotCelPEG (calculated in the same way as for the unmodified CD) was 3.2%.

The electronic and fluorescence properties of quantum dots semiconductors can be easily tuned through the size control of these nanoparticles. On the other hand, for carbon dots nanoparticles, these properties are mainly associated with the functional groups [29].

Also the presence of oxygenated functional groups, anchored at the edges of the basal planes of graphite structures decreases the electronic delocalization in the core, reducing its conductivity. In addition, some authors have shown that these same functional groups affect the density of states of the valence bands and increase the work function of multiwall carbon nanotubes [30]. For example, the functionalization of the graphene quantum dot (GQDs) by amino group decreases the band gap and increases the energy of the HOMO as a consequence of the charge redistribution. In the GQDs functionalized, the -NH2 group is positively charged due to the electron donation from the lone pair electrons of N to the antibonding state in the benzene ring of the structure [30]. For the carbon dots it has been verified that the reduction reaction with besides eliminating the oxygenated functional groups restores the π structure, that is, it increases the electronic density on the basal planes, reducing the band gap and shifting the photoluminescence to the blue [31] (see Supplementary Material, Fig. S3). Finally, all these observations suggest that increasing the electronic density on the core decreases the band gap and eliminates some surface traps or reduces the band bending degrees [32].
In this study, hydrophilic CD were prepared from eucalyptus cellulose pulp using sulfuric and nitric acid through a simple experimental setup.

The vigorous oxidation reaction which takes place by the use of the strong oxidant, i.e. HNO3, promotes the presence of a high amount of oxygenated functional groups on the surface of the obtained CD. The functional groups were characterized by different techniques showing the presence of mainly carboxylic acids (77% of the groups), besides lactones (14%), and phenolic groups (9%).

Optical properties of the obtained carbon dots showed that the peaks emission maxima shift linearly to higher wavelengths as the excitation wavelengths increase from 320 nm to 480 nm, and the quantum yield of the unmodified CD was ∼1.2%.

The great amount of carboxylic acid on the surface of the nanoparticles was used to passivate the obtained CD with oligomeric PEG1500 N molecules. The successfully passivation of the carbon nanoparticles with PEG molecules was confirmed by FTIR and optical properties, enhancing the quantum yield up to ∼3.2%.

Considering that the use of a renewable precursor such as cellulose and the use of a simple experimental setup can contribute to reduce the final cost of CD, we believe this work can contribute for the applications of these carbon nanoparticles in different areas such as bioimaging and photocatalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank Fapemig (CEX-APQ-00851-13 and EDT 02/2015 PPM IX) for financial support and Centro de Microscopia/UFMG.
